text: Smoking causes lung cancer, heart disease, and emphysema.
Cigarette smoke contains over 7,000 chemicals, hundreds of which are toxic.
Nicotine reaches the brain within 10 seconds of inhaling cigarette smoke.
Carbon monoxide in cigarette smoke pushes oxygen out of red blood cells.
Smoking damages the cilia in your lungs, making it harder to fight off infections.
Tar from cigarettes stains teeth and damages tooth enamel.
Smoking narrows and hardens blood vessels, raising the risk of heart attack and stroke.
Nicotine causes thinner and more brittle bones over time.
Smoking damages your DNA and blocks your body's ability to repair it.
Chemicals in cigarettes can damage the eyes and lead to vision loss.
Smoking weakens the immune system and increases the risk of autoimmune diseases.
Smoking during pregnancy can cause fetal injury, premature birth, and low birth weight.
Secondhand smoke causes serious health risks to non-smokers, including children.
Smoking can cause at least 12 different types of cancer.
Smoking causes type 2 diabetes, increasing risk by 30 to 40 percent compared to non-smokers.
Smoking causes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, known as COPD.
Nicotine dependence develops because the body builds a tolerance and needs more over time.
Withdrawal symptoms from quitting include anxiety, depression, restlessness, and insomnia.
Quitting smoking greatly reduces serious risks to your health at any age.
Within weeks of quitting, lung inflammation and mucus production begin to calm down.
Cilia in the lungs begin to regrow within a few months of quitting smoking.
Quitting smoking for several years can reduce cancer risk to nearly that of a non-smoker.
Nicotine replacement therapy options include patches, gum, lozenges, and nasal sprays.
Making a quit plan before stopping smoking greatly increases the chances of success.
Smoking causes premature aging of the skin, deepening wrinkles over time.
Smoking reduces the sense of smell by damaging nerve endings in the nose.
Children exposed to secondhand smoke are at higher risk for asthma and respiratory infections.
Smoking costs the United States over 300 billion dollars a year in healthcare expenses and lost productivity.
